channel.title: Tools on Open History Map
channel.description: Recent content in Tools on Open History Map

item.title: HTFS — Historical Transit Feed Specification
item.description:
GTFS describes today’s transit networks; HTFS is the equivalent draft for historical ones, where every line, stop and schedule comes with an explicit time-of-validity so a network can be reconstructed for any chosen date.

The repository is archived but the design is the basis for ongoing work on transit layers inside OHM, and the spec is the right starting point for anyone modelling historical mobility data.

item.title: Map Styles
item.description: A small repository of MapLibre / Mapbox-style JSON definitions used to render the OHM tile layers. Useful if you want to embed an OHM map in your own site, or fork a style as a starting point for a custom historical cartography.

item.title: OHM Map
item.description:
The OHM web client is the canonical entry point to the project’s data: a MapLibre-based interface that lets you pan and zoom across geography and across time, switching between historical layers, base maps and thematic overlays.

It is the public face of the platform; the rest of the stack on this page exists either to feed it data, or to extend the workflow around it.

item.title: OHM Zotero Plugin
item.description:
The OHM data pipeline is rooted in Zotero: each source — a paper, a map, a chronicle, a dataset — lives as a Zotero item, and OHM-specific metadata is carried as ohm:* tags on that item.

This plugin adds dedicated UI for editing those descriptors, so curators can work directly in Zotero rather than maintaining a parallel spreadsheet. Pair it with the Chrome extension below to capture sources from the web.

item.title: Tiles API
item.description:
The tiles API is the server that backs the map. Standard XYZ tile servers key tiles by (z, x, y); OHM data also has a temporal axis, so the API adds time-window filtering and per-layer grouping on top of the usual spatial pyramid.

It is the longest-running piece of OHM-specific infrastructure and underpins every map view we publish.

item.title: OHM Zotero Chrome Extension
item.description:
A small Chrome extension that lives next to the Zotero Connector and fills a specific gap: attaching arbitrary web pages as linked URL attachments to an item that already exists in your library.

Used together with the OHM Zotero plugin, it turns the everyday research browser into a one-click pipeline for enriching OHM source records.

item.title: Geocontext Front-End
item.description:
The companion to the QGIS plugin: a static front-end that reads a gcx.json manifest and renders the referenced GeoJSON datasets on an interactive map.

It is designed to be hosted alongside the bundle itself — drop the front-end and a Geocontext repository on the same host (or on GitHub Pages) and the bundle becomes a self-contained, shareable atlas.

item.title: Geocontext QGIS Plugin
item.description:
A QGIS plugin aimed at researchers who already work in QGIS and want to publish a slice of their project as an open, shareable bundle.

The plugin captures the current view — extents, layers and selected datasets — and writes them out as a gcx.json manifest plus datasets/*.geojson, then optionally pushes the result to a GitHub repository via the system git. The matching front-end (see Geocontext Front-End) renders these bundles back to a browser.

item.title: Historical Street View API
item.description:
Photography only goes back to the late 18th century, but humans have been making located views of the world for far longer. The Historical Street View project collects those views and lets you walk through them on a map, choosing both where and when you stand.

hsv-api is the back-end that serves those located views; visions.ofthepast.org is the public front-end built on top of it.
